A Living Family Museum

This Is What Happened

Entrance Hall

Every family leaves a story.

Some stories survive in records. Others live in photographs, treasured objects, and memories passed from one generation to the next. This museum gathers those fragments so the people who came before us are remembered as lives—not merely names and dates.

The Living Redwood

Every branch begins with the living family.

Four branches. One continuing story.

Blackwell Wing • Featured Ancestor Exhibit

Archive ID BW-000001

Thomas Emory Blackwell

31 January 1870 – 19 February 1962

Farmer, family storyteller, and the subject of one of the Blackwell family's most persistent legends.

“Some men leave records. Others leave legends.”

Thomas was born in Dent County, Missouri, married Eula Frances Dabney on 28 April 1892, raised a large family, and spent much of his life in Norman Township near Salem. His documented life appears ordinary on paper. Family memory describes a much wilder man.

Verified Record

Birth31 January 1870, Dent County, Missouri
Marriage28 April 1892 to Eula Frances Dabney
Death19 February 1962, Salem, Missouri
OccupationFarmer; later retired
BurialCedar Grove Cemetery, Salem, Missouri
ParentsJohn Smith Halbert Blackwell and Sarah Ellen Kincaid

The Man Behind the Records

Thomas spent roughly six decades in Norman Township and the Salem area. The 1950 census and his death certificate place him firmly in Dent County, Missouri. Family tradition remembers him as colorful, independent, and difficult to reduce to a list of dates.

Family Legends

The Missing Pinky

Family members say Thomas lost his little finger after being shot while serving in a war. The photograph of Thomas and Eula appears to show the missing finger.

Research statusFamily tradition with a visible photographic clue. The specific military conflict has not yet been identified.

Because Thomas was born in 1870, the Civil War cannot be the correct conflict. The story may refer to later military service, a militia action, or another firearms incident that became associated with “the war” as the story passed through generations.

The Jesse James Stories

Thomas was remembered as a man who told stories of running with Jesse James and robbing stagecoaches. Jesse James died in 1882, when Thomas was twelve, so the literal version of the story remains doubtful. The family memory may preserve a connection to former James-Younger associates, later outlaw culture, or Thomas's own flair for storytelling.

Research statusUnverified family legend. Preserve, investigate, and do not present as proven fact.

The Lost Blackwell Treasure

According to family tradition, Thomas buried money and valuables somewhere near his home on the Blackwell property. John Blackwell Sr. reportedly said Thomas could retrieve the cache in about twenty minutes.

No one found the treasure, and it was not for lack of trying. Search crews brought equipment to the property and treated the mystery like a local version of Oak Island.

The treasure remains unfound. Whether the cache existed or not, the search itself has become part of Blackwell family history.

Research statusLong-standing oral history supported by repeated family accounts; physical evidence has not been found.

Father and Son: A Family Contrast

Thomas Emory Blackwell

Farmer, adventurer in family memory, stagecoach storyteller, treasure legend, and a character whose stories survived for generations.

→

John Smith Blackwell Sr.

Baptist preacher, public servant, and reportedly a member of the Missouri House of Representatives—the apparent polar opposite of his father.

Evidence Gallery

Thomas and Eula

The family photograph in which Thomas's injured hand can be examined.

Missouri Death Certificate, 1962

Documents his birth, death, occupation, parents, residence, cause of death, and burial.

1950 United States Census

Places Thomas and Eula in Dent County and records household and occupation information.

Gravestone

Thomas E. Blackwell and Eula F. Blackwell, with marriage date preserved on the marker.

Genealogy Summary

A current biographical summary requiring comparison with original records before final museum citation.

Portrait of Thomas

A preserved family image used as the central portrait for this exhibit.

Research Questions

Military Service

Locate enlistment, muster, pension, militia, or veterans records that may explain the missing finger.

Outlaw Connection

Search Dent County newspapers, court records, oral histories, and associates connected to the James-Younger era.

Property & Treasure

Map Thomas's land, home site, outbuildings, wells, caves, and later search locations.

Public-Service Legacy

Document John Blackwell Sr.'s ministry and service in the Missouri House of Representatives.

Curator's standard: Documents are displayed as evidence. Family legends are preserved because they shaped the family's memory, but they remain clearly labeled until supporting records are found.

Every object receives a permanent museum identity.

Documents

Archive Numbers

ZA, DW, BW, and BA collection numbering

Research Center

The museum distinguishes known facts from unfinished research.

The archive preserves what is known while leaving room for what remains undiscovered. Incomplete dates and unanswered questions are kept visible so future generations can continue the search.

The work continues: every new record, photograph, place, and remembered story adds another layer to the family’s history.
